coredata.dc:title: Bright-yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift for selective fluorescent detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol in environmental water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengrui
Wang, Huajie
Li, Yunyun
Data, Fengkun Yang
Wang, Qin
Jiao, Long
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) has been constructed for selective and sensitive sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). Y-CDs with a large Stokes shift (115 nm) were obtained through a simple one-pot solvothermal method. Significantly, the addition of TNP induced remarkable quenching of fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs via an inner filter effect. There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence of Y-CDs and TNP concentration (0.2–130 μM), displaying a limit of detection of 56 nM. The nanoprobe can selectively detect TNP without interference from other nitroaromatic explosives, and has been successfully used for determining TNP in environmental water samples, representing a convenient tool for TNP sensing in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Sensors
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of the Y-CDs for TNP
Determination of TNP in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of Y-CDs and their application for TNP sensing.
TEM (A) and histograms of the particle size distribution (B) images of Y-CDs; XPS survey (C) and FT-IR characterization (D) of the structure and functional groups of Y-CDs.

(A) pH-dependent fluorescence spectral changes of Y-CDs (λex = 450 nm) at 565 nm vs pH; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs under different excitation wavelengths; (C) Fluorescence responses of Y-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of TNP (from top to bottom, 0, 0.2, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 45, 70, 90, 110, 130 μM) in a phosphate buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4). Inset: photographs of Y-CDs in the absence (left) and presence (right) of TNP under UV light (365 nm). (D) F 0/F value of Y-CDs as a function of the concentration of TNP. F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively.
Determination of TNP in environmental water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Bright-yellow-emissive carbon dots with a large Stokes shift for selective fluorescent detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol in environmental water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Shiyou University, Xi'an 710065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Shiyou University, Xi'an 710065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent nanoprobe based on yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) has been constructed for selective and sensitive sensing of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). Y-CDs with a large Stokes shift (115 nm) were obtained through a simple one-pot solvothermal method. Significantly, the addition of TNP induced remarkable quenching of fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs via an inner filter effect. There was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence of Y-CDs and TNP concentration (0.2–130 μM), displaying a limit of detection of 56 nM. The nanoprobe can selectively detect TNP without interference from other nitroaromatic explosives, and has been successfully used for determining TNP in environmental water samples, representing a convenient tool for TNP sensing in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: 2, 4, 6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), a nitroaromatic, is widely used in industry and chemical laboratories. However, the wide use of TNP may contaminate groundwater and cause many serious diseases, such as cancer, abnormal liver functioning and so on [1]. For this reason, many fluorescent indicators, especially nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors, have been used to monitor TNP, such as silicon nanoparticles, CuInS2 quantum dots, N-doped graphene quantum dots, and Ag @ metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [2–5]. However, there are several disadvantages of these TNP indicators that cannot be ignored, such as time-consuming synthesis procedures and small Stokes shifts, limiting their potential application. Therefore, more effort should be devoted to constructing nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors for TNP measurements.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) were applied for TNP detection due to their high photostability, excellent solubility, and favorable biocompatibility [6,7]. However, most of these nanoprobes suffer from the general problem of short Stokes shift and wavelength emission, which may cause severe photodamage to living organisms, and the auto-fluorescence of the biological matrix [8,9]. To overcome these shortcomings, CD-based TNP sensors with higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and longer emissive wavelength have been investigated by some groups in recent years [6,10]. However, the exploration of CD-based TNP nanoprobes with long-wavelength emission and a large Stokes shift remains at an early stage.

Toward this end, we synthesized bright-yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) with excellent fluorescence emission at 565 nm (λex = 450 nm) as fluorescent TNP sensors. The prepared Y-CDs were obtained through one-pot solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and urea, and display a large Stokes shift (115 nm). The fluorescent intensity of Y-CDs could be quenched by TNP due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between TNP and Y-CDs (Fig. 1 ). Moreover, the nanosensor showed excellent sensitivity and specificity toward TNP over other nitroaromatic explosives and has been successfully applied in detecting TNP changes in environmental water samples.
Materials, instruments, and other general experimental methods. For details, see Supporting Information.

Briefly, 100 mg of o-PD and urea were dissolved in 10 mL of C2H5OH. Then, the solution was subsequently transferred into a 25 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained solution was purified via a silica column chromatography using a mixture of methylene dichloride and methanol as the eluent (CH2Cl2/CH3OH, 20: 1 v/v). After evaporating the solvent, the Y-CD sample was obtained.

Ten milligrams of Y-CDs was dissolved in 10 mL of C2H5OH to obtain the stock solution. In a set of test tubes, 0.5 mL phosphate buffer (40 mM, pH 7.4), 20 μL of Y-CDs (1 mg/mL) and different concentrations of TNP were added. The reaction mixture was diluted to 1.0 mL with phosphate buffer and mixed. Then, the fluorescence spectra or intensity was recorded with excitation at 450 nm.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2 A) shows that Y-CDs are spherical in shape, and their size ranges from 1.4 to 3.5 nm, with an average size of 2.5 nm (calculated based on the measurement of more than 100 particles) (Fig. 2B). Subsequently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated that carbon, oxygen and nitrogen are the main components of the CDs (Fig. 2C). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) characterization shows that the Y-CDs possess –NH2 (3135 cm−1), aliphatic CH2 (2817 cm−1), CO (1756 cm−1), –CN (1482 cm−1), and C–N–C (1208 cm−1) functional groups or chemical bonds (Fig. 2D). The XPS and FT-IR analyses are consistent and provide convincing evidence that –NH2 groups are present on the surface of Y-CDs, which is a suitable chemically reactive group for further functionalization and interaction with analytes [11].

The obtained Y-CD solution exhibits a long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature and no obvious fluorescence intensity changes were observed even after 36 h (Fig. S1). Furthermore, strong fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was obtained in the pH range of 7.4–8.3 (Fig. 3 A). Clearly, Y-CDs are ideal for constructing TNP nanoprobes in aqueous solution. Moreover, the maximum emission wavelength (excited at 450 nm) was 565 nm, and the fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs are λex-independent (when the excitation wavelength is changed from 340 to 490 nm, the emission wavelength showed nearly no shift) (Fig. 3B), which could be conducive to their application by avoiding undesired autofluorescence phenomenon [12]; this excitation-independent behavior was attributed to the chemical composition of CDs rather than their morphology [13]. The absolute QY of Y-CDs was measured to be 56%, which is very high and can effectively overcome the autofluorescence of cells.

Next, the capability of Y-CDs to detect TNP was examined by introducing TNP to a solution of Y-CDs in phosphate buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4). Upon progressive addition of TNP (0–130 μM), a color change could be observed under UV light (365 nm, insert) and the fluorescence intensity of Y-CD solution at 565 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 3C). The TNP-induced fluorescence quenching of Y-CDs was considered to be caused by an inner filter effect (IFE) because there was a large spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of TNP and the excitation spectrum of the fluorescent Y-CDs (Fig. S2) [2,13]. More importantly, it was confirmed that the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm is linear against TNP concentrations in the range of 0–130 μM with a limit of detection of 56 nM (3σ) (Fig. 3D). These results demonstrate that Y-CDs could be applied as a new kind of nanoprobe for TNP sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the Y-CDs for TNP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the nanoprobe, Y-CDs were incubated with other compounds, such as inorganic ions and other nitroaromatics that may exist in environmental samples. No distinct fluorescence quenching was triggered by the addition of those compounds (Fig. S3). Competition experiments were performed by adding TNP to the Y-CD solution in the presence of the above compounds (Fig. S3). No obvious emission intensity changes were presented compared to that of the system containing only TNP, suggesting that the nanoprobe has excellent selectivity for TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TNP in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the toxicity of TNP, the Y-CD sensor was tested to validate its practical utility in environmental science. Water samples were collected from the Yellow River, Yangtze River, and tap water in Hanzhong City. As shown in Table 1 , the method showed good recovery values, ranging from 97.0 to 103.0%, and the RSD was below 2.3%. These results suggested that the nanoprobe possesses the potential application for detecting TNP in environmental water samples.

In conclusion, Y-CDs with a remarkably large Stokes shift were obtained through a simple one-pot solvothermal carbonization method and were successfully used as a nanoprobe for detecting TNP. The obvious fluorescence quenching of Y-CDs with increased TNP concentration could be ascribed to the IFE mechanism between TNP and Y-CDs. The nanosensor has been successfully applied for the precise measurement of TNP in environmental water samples with good recoveries (97.0–103.0%). Therefore, this method provides a promising nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection and quantification of the highly toxic TNP found in environmental samples.
Shengrui Zhang: Investigation. Huajie Wang: . Yunyun Li: . Fengkun Yang Data: . Qin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Long Jiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21807068, 21775118), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (No. 2018JM2018), the Scientific Research Plan Projects of Shaanxi Education Department of China (19JK0192), Projects of Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students in Shaanxi Province (S201910720049).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.127208.
